questions.explanation: The narrator observes, "One thing late or early can disrupt everything around it, and the disturbance runs outward in bands like the waves from a dropped stone in a quiet pool." Placed at the homecoming, this thematizes how a single disruptive act—Cal's revenge after his rejection—will ripple outward to wreck the whole family. The train itself arrives "On time," so it is not about lateness or mere technology.

questions.question: Trace Cal's internal struggle in section 2 from his jealousy through the "new voice" that enters his mind. What claim can you make about his moral character at this point, and what specific evidence in the passage supports it?
questions.modelAnswer: Cal honestly names his worst motives—admitting "It's just jealousy," that he is trying to "buy" his father, and that there is "not one decent thing about me." A defensible claim is that Cal possesses genuine self-awareness and a conscience, but is not yet in control of himself. The evidence is the "new voice" that coolly exposes even his self-punishment as a kind of enjoyment and self-protection ("By whipping himself he protected himself against whipping by someone else"). His resolve to "give it lightly" and "Give the day to Aron" shows he can recognize the good, yet his self-knowledge alone does not guarantee he will choose it.
questions.explanation: Section 2 shows Cal interrogating himself: "I'm jealous... I don't want to be jealous," then accusing himself of trying to buy his father, then a contemptuous "new voice" revealing he enjoys the self-beating as armor. A strong answer makes a defensible claim about his conscience and lack of control and cites at least two of these specific moments rather than summarizing plot.

questions.question: Adam tells Cal, "If you want to give me a present—give me a good life," and Lee insists Cal still "has a choice." Evaluate Cal's response to these moments in light of the novel's good-versus-evil and timshel throughline. Does Cal exercise his choice well? Support your judgment with textual evidence.
questions.modelAnswer: Cal is offered the central freedom of timshel—the choice to rule over his impulse rather than be ruled by it—and he fails to exercise it well. After Adam's rejection, hate "seeping all through his body" overwhelms him and his brain reaches "an aching triumph," depicted in the spirals shrinking to a single "shiny black dot" of cruel intent. When Lee pleads, "You have a choice," Cal denies even understanding him and mockingly says, "You'd think... I'd killed somebody," then seeks out Aron to "show" him something. A defensible judgment is that Cal chooses revenge over mastery, embodying the chapter's warning that the freedom timshel grants can be turned toward evil as well as good.
questions.explanation: The throughline asks whether a person, given the choice, can master sin. Adam's wish for "a good life" and Lee's "You have a choice" present Cal that choice; Cal rejects it (denying Lee, the spirals collapsing to a black dot, walking off to find Aron). A strong evaluative answer renders a clear verdict and grounds it in these specific lines and images rather than offering only opinion.

chapters.summary: Shaken by Cal's visit with Aron the night before, Kate sits paralyzed while Joe Valery tries to manipulate her, only to expose himself; she retaliates by mailing a note that will get his fingerprints checked. Drifting into childhood memories of Alice in Wonderland and her 'Drink me' bottle, Kate writes a will leaving everything to Aron, then takes poison and dies, shrinking 'smaller and smaller' until 'she had never been.' Joe ransacks her room, finds her blackmail cache, and is shot dead while fleeing the sheriff's man.

questions.question: In the two documents Kate writes this chapter, what does she set in motion against Joe and arrange for Aron?
options: A note urging the sheriff to check Joe's fingerprints and a will leaving everything to her son Aron
A confession naming Joe as a blackmailer and a will leaving the house to her girls
A check to buy Joe's silence and a letter warning Aron to stay away
A note firing Joe and a will dividing her estate between Aron and Cal
questions.explanation: Kate writes to Ralph, 'Tell the sheriff it wouldn't do any harm to check on Joe Valery's fingerprints,' and later forces her hand to write 'I leave everything I have to my son Aron Trask,' signed 'Catherine Trask.' She neither bargains with Joe nor includes Cal.

questions.question: How does Joe Valery die at the end of the chapter?
options: Oscar Noble shoots him as he runs toward an alley while fleeing the sheriff
He poisons himself with the same capsule Kate used
He is stabbed in a brawl with the college boys at the house
The sheriff arrests him and he hangs himself in his cell
questions.explanation: Taken to see the sheriff, Joe bolts: 'Joe ran like a rabbit.' Oscar braces against a telephone pole and fires, and 'Joe splashed forward on his face,' a crowd gathering around the body.

questions.question: Why does the alarm Joe causes—when Kate realizes he has been digging into Faye's history—paradoxically bring Kate 'something like pleasure'?
options: Catching and outwitting Joe gives her a concrete enemy to fight, distracting her from the deeper despair Cal triggered
She has secretly wanted to be exposed for Faye's death and welcomes the chance
She believes Joe's slip proves Ethel is finally dead and no longer a threat
She is delighted to have found a loyal accomplice she can finally trust
questions.explanation: The text says 'The sudden alarm was good for Kate. It took the blond head and the pain from her. It gave her something to do. She responded to the challenge with something like pleasure.' Sparring with Joe is a winnable contest that briefly displaces the unmanageable wound of Cal and Aron.

questions.question: What does Kate's childhood memory of the Alice in Wonderland 'Drink me' bottle reveal about the meaning of her death?
options: Her suicide is a lifelong fantasy of vanishing made literal—escaping enemies by shrinking until she 'never would have been'
It shows she has finally found religious faith and expects a peaceful afterlife
It proves she killed herself impulsively, without any prior thought of escape
It reveals she intends to fake her death and disappear to start a new life
questions.explanation: As a girl, Kate kept a 'Drink me' bottle as 'her threat and her safety'—'when she stopped being, she never would have been.' Her death enacts this: she says 'Eat me' and 'Drink me,' grows 'smaller and smaller and then disappeared—and she had never been.'

questions.question: When Cal's face hangs in Kate's mind saying, 'You missed something. They had something and you missed it,' what realization is breaking her?
options: That other people possess a goodness or wholeness she lacks and can never name
That Cal has stolen money she had hidden in the house
That she should have raised her sons herself instead of abandoning them
That Aron is the weaker son and will not avenge her
questions.explanation: Kate insists 'She was smarter and stronger than other people. She had something they lacked,' but the thought collapses into its inverse: 'They had something she lacked, and she didn't know what it was.' Cal's imagined taunt fixes this absence as what she 'missed.'

questions.question: How does Steinbeck shape the reader's response to Kate in her final hours so that her death is not simply triumphant villainy defeated?
options: By exposing her loneliness, pain, and frightened-child memories, he lets pity complicate the reader's judgment of her evil
By having her repent fully and ask God's forgiveness before dying
By proving every cruel act she committed was actually justified self-defense
By making the surrounding characters mourn her as a beloved community figure
questions.explanation: Steinbeck dwells on Kate 'cold and desolate, alone and desolate,' her aching crooked hands, and the 'cut-off little girl' hiding from a 'tree-tall forest of enemies.' This vulnerability invites pity alongside revulsion, refusing a clean moral verdict even as her death removes the novel's incarnation of evil.

questions.question: Kate dies convinced that other people 'had something she lacked.' Connect this realization to the novel's good-versus-evil and timshel throughline: what does Steinbeck suggest Kate 'missed,' and how does the chapter dramatize her inability to attain it? Make a claim and support it with specific textual evidence.
questions.modelAnswer: Claim: Steinbeck frames what Kate 'missed' as the human capacity for goodness and love—the very thing timshel ('thou mayest') says people may choose. The chapter dramatizes that she cannot reach it. Evidence: she insists 'She was smarter and stronger than other people. She had something they lacked,' yet the thought inverts into 'They had something she lacked, and she didn't know what it was,' and Cal's imagined face confirms 'You missed something.' Her only refuge is the childhood fantasy of vanishing toward Alice rather than toward any human bond, suggesting that here evil is less a defiant choice than a tragic incapacity—the dark counterpoint to the novel's hope that people may choose good.
questions.explanation: Grounded in Kate's collapsing self-assertion ('They had something she lacked'), Cal's refrain 'You missed something. They had something and you missed it,' and her retreat into the 'Drink me' Alice fantasy rather than toward any person—tying her death to the timshel/good-vs-evil throughline.

questions.question: Steinbeck cuts between Kate's ritualized death and Joe's frantic scavenging and flight. In what way does pairing these two endings deepen the chapter's meaning? Make a claim about the contrast or parallel and support it with details from the text.
questions.modelAnswer: Claim: Juxtaposing Kate's interior, almost mythic death with Joe's grubby, opportunistic scramble both contrasts and parallels them as two forms of human emptiness destroyed by their own scheming. Evidence: Kate dies turned inward toward Alice and oblivion, shrinking until 'she had never been'; immediately after, Joe rifles her body and desk for the gold watch, money, and the 'gold mine' of blackmail envelopes, caring only for what he can grab. The parallel is that both predators are undone—Kate's own fingerprint note dooms Joe, who runs 'like a rabbit' and is shot minutes after gloating over his find. The tonal contrast underscores that Kate's evil carried tragic weight that Joe's petty greed wholly lacks.
questions.explanation: Grounded in the cut from Kate's death ('she had never been') to Joe's robbery (watch, safe-deposit keys 27 and 29, the will, the blackmail photographs—'Look at that fat-ass councilman!') and his being shot while fleeing, the consequence of the very note Kate mailed.

chapters.summary: Sheriff Quinn closes out Kate's death—burning the blackmail photographs from her safe-deposit box and bringing Adam her will, which leaves over a hundred thousand dollars to Aron, the son who does not even know she was his mother. Meanwhile Cal, sick with guilt for having taken Aron to see Kate, drinks himself blind and burns the money he had given his father, until Lee breaks through his self-pity; the chapter ends as Adam, showing the first signs of a stroke, cannot read the postcard announcing that Aron has lied about his age and run off to enlist in the army.

questions.question: What news does the government postcard at the end of the chapter deliver to Adam?
options: That Aron has lied about his age and enlisted in the army
That Aron has been killed in the war
That Kate has left her estate to Aron
That Cal has been drafted into service
questions.explanation: The chapter closes with Adam unable to read a yellow government postcard, which Lee reads aloud: 'Dear Father, I'm in the army. I told them I was eighteen... Aron.' Aron has run off and enlisted, not died, and it is this card Adam cannot read as a stroke comes on.

questions.question: What physical signs in this chapter indicate that Adam is beginning to suffer a stroke?
options: He faints at the post office, his hand goes numb, and he cannot read the postcard
He sets out at once to enlist himself
He destroys his refrigeration equipment in a rage
He recovers his eyesight and weeps with relief
questions.explanation: After learning of Kate's suicide and going to the post office, Adam faints (a 'tiny sharp incredibly bright blue light flashed' behind his eyes earlier), his left hand goes numb, his speech is slow 'like a dream talker,' and the letters on the postcard 'jump around' so he cannot read it—classic onset of a stroke, not a collapse over news of Aron's death.

questions.question: Cal's anguish throughout the chapter is rooted chiefly in which conviction about himself?
options: That his cruelty in taking Aron to see their mother makes him responsible for Aron running away
That he failed to make enough money to win his father's love
That he is destined to inherit Kate's business against his will
That Abra will reject him for someone more respectable
questions.explanation: Cal is tormented by guilt: going to Kate's 'had been no relief from pain and his revenge had been no triumph,' Aron struck him and ran off 'screaming like a brokenhearted child,' and Cal knows he 'had to bring Aron back.' His suffering is over the harm he did Aron, not money, inheritance, or romance.

questions.question: Why does Lee mock Cal as 'Caleb the magnificent' whose 'suffering should have its Homer'?
options: To puncture Cal's self-dramatizing despair so he can stop wallowing and begin to recover
To confirm that Cal has inherited Kate's evil and must accept it
To urge Cal to flee the house and never return
To blame himself entirely and refuse to speak to Cal further
questions.explanation: Lee deliberately needles Cal—accusing him of trying to 'attract dignity and tragedy' to himself and of wanting 'the eminence of being a murderer'—to break his self-pity. The text says Lee 'had worked so hard, so tenderly,' watching for relief 'the way a doctor watches for the reaction to a hypodermic,' and the provocation succeeds in starting Cal's recovery.

questions.question: What does Sheriff Quinn do with the photographs he finds among Kate's effects, and why?
options: He burns them in his office stove because they could 'tear the county to pieces' through blackmail
He files them as evidence to convict Kate's associates
He mails copies to each man pictured as a warning
He gives them to Adam so Adam can destroy them himself
questions.explanation: Quinn calls the envelopes 'a deck of hell' that could cause 'half a dozen suicides,' then burns them in the iron stove and poles the ash to powder. He sends a lawyer to tell every man on his list the pictures are destroyed—an act of mercy he knows will cost him the goodwill of guilt-ridden men and likely his office.

questions.question: What is the significance of Cal burning the money in this chapter?
options: It is the money Adam had refused, and burning it is an act of self-punishment and attempted sacrifice for Aron
It is Kate's inheritance, which Cal destroys to keep it from Aron
It is counterfeit currency Cal is disposing of before the sheriff arrives
It is Adam's savings, which Cal burns out of revenge against his father
questions.explanation: Cal takes 'the flat package' of bills he had given his father (and which Adam rejected) and burns them one by one, the flame reaching his fingertips before he drops each. The narration ties this to guilt and 'the idea of sacrifice,' which 'took hold of him the way it does with all guilty-feeling men'—a self-inflicted punishment, not revenge or concealment.

questions.question: How does Lee's confrontation with Cal in his room dramatize the novel's idea that a guilty person is still free to choose his path? Identify Cal's mistaken belief and explain how Lee's method works on him.
questions.modelAnswer: Cal believes he is simply 'mean'—doomed by his nature and his mother's blood—and indulges this belief by burning the money and savoring his despair, even hoping to claim 'the eminence of being a murderer' if Aron is harmed. Lee refuses to flatter that self-image; he names Cal's cruelty plainly ('You were just mean') but insists the cause was ordinary hurt, not destiny, and mocks Cal's tragic posing to deflate it. By provoking rage, then hurt, then relief 'the way a doctor watches for the reaction to a hypodermic,' Lee leads Cal past self-pity toward responsibility and the possibility of changing—the timshel idea that guilt is real but does not abolish moral freedom. Cal's grateful half-smile shows he recognizes the 'well-done job' and is no longer trapped in his fatalism.
questions.explanation: The scene is grounded in Lee's deliberate goading and Cal's plea 'I don't want to be mean. Help me, Lee!' The text frames Cal's meanness as a choice tied to hurt feelings rather than an inescapable inheritance, restating the book's doctrine that one who has done evil can still turn.

questions.question: Sheriff Quinn appears throughout this chapter as a man near the end of his career. How does Steinbeck use Quinn's reflections and his handling of Kate's photographs to develop the theme that doing right often brings sorrow rather than reward? Support your answer with the text.
questions.modelAnswer: Quinn recalls a doctor's remark that delivering a baby brings 'joy at the end of it,' and reflects that in his own work, 'if he did his work well there was sorrow at the end of it for somebody.' He acts decisively and humanely—burning the blackmail photographs that could ruin the county and having the men told they are safe—yet he knows the very men he protects will hate him, that 'part of the curse was for him,' and that these 'guilt-feeling men' will push him out of office. His visit to Adam likewise brings grief, breaking the news of Kate's suicide and producing the will. Through Quinn, Steinbeck shows that moral action is not rewarded with gratitude; the right thing leaves a residue of shame and resentment in others, and the doer carries the sorrow alone.
questions.explanation: Grounded in Quinn's stove-burning of the photos, his certainty that the protected men will hate him and end his tenure, and his musing that good work yields 'sorrow at the end of it,' the chapter develops the cost of conscience—right action bringing pain rather than thanks.

chapters.summary: Set in the bleak winter of 1917-1918 as the war goes badly for the Allies, Chapter 52 finds Adam gravely diminished after the shock of Aron's departure—his hand numb, his eyes failing, dependent on Lee—while Cal is consumed by guilt over having shown Aron their mother. The chapter's heart is Cal's confession to Abra, who reveals she long ago knew the truth and had already stopped loving Aron because he clung to a childhood "story" he refused to let reality change; she tells Cal she loves him precisely because he is not "good," and quietly burns Aron's letters.

questions.question: What is Adam Trask's condition in this chapter, following the shock of Aron's departure?
options: He is physically diminished—his left hand is numb, his eyes will not focus, and he is dizzy and dependent on Lee
He has fully recovered and has returned to active work on the draft board
He has left Salinas to search for Aron at his army training camp
He is bedridden with the same influenza that is sweeping the town
questions.explanation: Adam is given a leave of absence for ill health; he rubs and soaks his numb left hand, cannot tie his own shoes, feels too dizzy to walk without bracing against a wall, and cannot read because the words 'jumble all around.' Lee must help him up and read to him.

questions.question: Why does Lee resist Cal's suggestion that they have Adam's eyes tested?
options: He suspects the problem is not really Adam's eyes and fears that finding out the truth would trouble Adam, who has had a bad shock
He believes the eye doctor in Salinas is incompetent and untrustworthy
He thinks Adam is faking the illness to get sympathy and attention
He wants to wait until Aron returns so the family can go together
questions.explanation: Lee tells Cal, 'I don't think it's his eyes. Finding out might trouble him. Let him be for a while. He's had a bad shock.' He instead hopes Dr. Edwards might drop by for a 'friendly call,' suggesting Lee senses Adam's trouble is more serious than vision.

questions.question: During his confession to Abra, what does Cal admit he did to Aron, and when did it happen?
options: On Thanksgiving night he took Aron to see their mother, the madam, and Aron went mad, knocked him down, and ran away
That very morning he had forged a letter to drive Aron out of college
He had stolen the money Aron saved and spent it on Kate's brothel
He had told their father a lie that turned Adam against Aron
questions.explanation: Cal tells Abra: 'Our mother was a whore... Thanksgiving night I took Aron down and showed her to him.' He says Aron 'went mad—just crazy,' yelled at her, then 'knocked me down and ran away.' The reveal is recounted as a past event, not something occurring in this chapter.

questions.question: Abra explains that she stopped loving Aron. According to her, what was the root cause?
options: Aron clung to an idealized childhood 'story' and could not bear to let reality change how it came out
Aron had grown cruel and began to treat her coldly and possessively
She had fallen in love with Cal long before and felt she was deceiving Aron
Aron's decision to become a priest proved he was too devout for an ordinary marriage
questions.explanation: Abra says that as children they 'lived in a story,' but when she grew up 'the story wasn't true any more,' while 'Aron didn't grow up... He wanted the story and he wanted it to come out his way.' He 'couldn't stand to know about his mother because that's not how he wanted the story to go.'

questions.question: When Abra tells Cal 'I think I love you' and he protests 'I'm not good,' she answers 'Because you're not good.' What does her reply most reveal about her view of human worth?
options: She values honesty and the capacity to face reality over a 'goodness' that depends on denying it
She believes badness is more exciting and is drawn to Cal's capacity for cruelty
She is mocking Cal and does not actually mean that she loves him
She thinks goodness is impossible, so she has given up expecting it in anyone
questions.explanation: Throughout the scene Abra contrasts Aron—who tore up the world rather than revise his illusions—with Cal, who can face ugly truth. Her embrace of Cal 'because' he is not 'good' affirms that she prizes self-aware engagement with reality over a brittle purity built on denial, echoing the novel's reckoning with timshel and chosen morality.

questions.question: The closing section follows Abra in her own home. What does her behavior there—tiptoeing past her father's door, hiding letters, and recognizing her father is 'hiding from something'—chiefly reveal?
options: She is perceptive and quietly independent, seeing through adult pretenses while outwardly obeying her controlling mother
She is frightened of her parents and plans to run away to follow Aron
She is as deluded as Aron, inventing comforting stories about her family
She resents Cal and is destroying his letters in a fit of jealousy
questions.explanation: Abra has 'given up having any private things' under a mother who dresses and plans for her, yet 'her privacies were of the mind.' She knows 'without question' that her father is not ill but 'hiding from something,' and that Adam is genuinely ill—showing a quiet, discerning maturity. The letters she burns are Aron's, not Cal's.

questions.question: In this chapter Abra articulates a distinction between Aron's way of facing the world and Cal's. Drawing on the specific things she says to Cal, evaluate how Steinbeck uses Abra's growth to critique a 'goodness' built on denial—and how this reframes the reader's sympathies between the two brothers.
questions.modelAnswer: Abra explains that as children she and Aron 'lived in a story,' but that when she grew up 'the story wasn't enough' because 'it wasn't true any more,' whereas Aron 'didn't grow up' and insisted the story 'come out his way.' She frames Aron's flight from the truth about his mother as a refusal to let reality intrude on his ideal—'he tore up the world' rather than accept it—the same rigidity by which he 'tore me up' when he wanted to be a priest. Steinbeck uses her hard-won maturity (others stop being 'ghosts furnished for you to talk to') to expose Aron's purity as a brittle self-centeredness that cannot survive contact with reality. This reframes the reader's earlier sympathy for the 'good' son: Abra's choice of Cal—loving him 'because' he is not good—suggests that the capacity to face ugliness honestly is a deeper virtue than an innocence that depends on denial.
questions.explanation: A strong answer cites Abra's 'story' metaphor and her statement that Aron refused to let the story 'come out any other way,' connects his flight to a self-centered refusal of reality ('he tore up the world'), and shows how her maturation (people ceasing to be 'ghosts') critiques denial-based goodness and shifts sympathy toward the flawed but reality-facing Cal.

questions.question: This chapter dramatizes guilt and its aftermath without re-staging the brothel revelation itself. Analyze how Steinbeck handles Cal's guilt through his exchanges with Lee and his confession to Abra, and what this reveals about Cal's moral condition at this point in the novel.
questions.modelAnswer: Steinbeck keeps the actual reveal offstage—it surfaces only as Cal's retrospective confession—so the chapter's focus is on consequence rather than the act. With Lee, Cal's guilt manifests as self-punishment ('Maybe I deserve to be killed'), which Lee sharply rejects as 'the cheapest kind of self-indulgence,' insisting Cal must eventually 'open up' and tell Adam 'for yourself.' Cal's bitterness toward Aron ('The son of a bitch') and his fear of Abra show guilt curdling into defensiveness, yet his voluntary, unflinching confession to her—naming himself 'mean' and 'dirty' and expecting her to walk away—reveals a conscience actively at work. His moral condition is therefore unstable but not damned: he knows the gravity of what he did and is suffering for it, and the chapter positions him on the edge of either being crushed by guilt or being redeemed through honest reckoning, consistent with the novel's timshel theme that he may yet choose his path.
questions.explanation: A strong answer notes that the reveal is handled in retrospect; reads Cal's 'I deserve to be killed' and Lee's rebuke about self-indulgence as the chapter's treatment of guilt; observes that Lee withholds confession to Adam until he recovers; and interprets Cal's frank confession to Abra as evidence of a working conscience that leaves his moral fate open under the timshel theme.

chapters.summary: Abra visits Lee bearing a potato peeler as a gift, and the two openly declare a father-daughter love, with Lee giving her his mother's jade button carved with a hand. Abra reveals she has burned all of Aron's letters and is freeing herself from his demand that she be perfect, while Adam declines into frequent sleep and tearful confusion about his father being a thief. Cal, newly reformed and drilling in his uniform, walks Abra home and afterward wanders the cold deserted town, thinking of leaving flowers on his mother Kate's grave.

questions.question: What gift does Abra bring to Lee when she comes to visit?
options: A new kind of potato peeler that takes off just the skin
A carved ebony box with a dragon on it
A jade button carved with a human hand
A box of strawberry tarts
questions.explanation: Abra shows Cal a little cardboard box and says, "It's a new kind of potato peeler. Takes off just the skin... I got it for Lee." The ebony box and the jade button are gifts Lee gives to Abra, not the other way around, and the tarts are what Lee is baking.

questions.question: What change in Adam's condition does the chapter establish at the start of section 2?
options: He has recovered his strength and plans to return to work full-time
He has taken to sleeping very often, in short sleeps day and night, and struggles with buttons and shoelaces
He has gone completely blind and can no longer leave his bed
He has become violently angry and refuses to let Lee near him
questions.explanation: The text states Adam "had taken to sleeping very often—short sleeps during the night and during the day," and that "Buttons and shoelaces and getting things on frontways gave Adam trouble." His decline is shown through tears coming suddenly and confusion about his father, not through anger or total blindness, though he does mention having Victor look at his eyes.

questions.question: When Lee tells Abra, "And now that you don't have to be perfect, you can be good," what does her decision to burn Aron's letters reveal about her relationship with Aron?
options: She had stopped loving Aron entirely and now hated him for joining the army
She felt Aron's idealized image of her forced her to perform a goodness she could not feel, and freeing herself from it lets her be genuinely good
She burned the letters out of jealousy because Aron had written to another girl
She destroyed the letters to hide evidence of a secret engagement from her parents
questions.explanation: Abra explains, "Lately I never felt good enough. I always wanted to explain to him that I was not good." Aron loved an idealized Abra, and Lee names the insight directly: released from the burden of being perfect, she can now be authentically good. The other options invent motives (hatred, jealousy, hidden engagement) the text does not support.

questions.question: Lee privately reflects that Adam lived "all his life on stolen money" and Aron lived "on the profits from a whorehouse." What larger idea is Lee entertaining through these reflections?
options: That Adam and Aron were secretly criminals who deserved punishment
That money is the root of all the family's suffering and should be given away
That a moral balance may operate, so that if life tilts too far one way an automatic correction restores equilibrium
That Sam Hamilton was the only truly honest man the family ever knew
questions.explanation: Lee wonders whether "things balance so that if one went too far in one direction an automatic slide moved on the scale and the balance was re-established." The irony of the rigidly honest Adam and the self-indulgently pure Aron unknowingly living on tainted money prompts a meditation on moral equilibrium, not a literal accusation or a plan to give money away.

questions.question: When the constable Tom Meek asks Cal what he did with the fifteen thousand dollars and Cal answers, "I burned it up," why does Tom refuse to believe him?
options: Tom knows Cal is lying because Will Hamilton told him Cal still had the money
The truth is so unexpected that Tom assumes Cal is kidding him, which he resents
Tom thinks Cal gave the money to Aron to help him in the army
Cal whispers the answer so quietly that Tom mishears him
questions.explanation: Cal tells the literal truth—he "set a match to it and burned it"—but Tom responds, "Oh, yeah! Sure. Good thing to do," and walks off muttering, "The young punk son of a bitch... He's getting too smart for himself," because Tom "didn't like people to kid him." The genuine confession is dismissed precisely because it is too strange to be taken seriously.

questions.question: At the chapter's close Cal imagines putting flowers on Kate's grave and recalls that carnations are the "Nails of Love" and marigolds the "Nails of Death," deciding "maybe he'd better put marigolds." What does this final image most reveal about Cal's inner state?
options: That Cal has decided to forgive and idealize his mother completely
That Cal is wrestling with how to feel about his dead mother, hovering between tenderness and a colder acknowledgment of death
That Cal plans to desecrate the grave as revenge for Aron's death
That Cal has become as self-righteously pure as Aron and rejects his mother entirely
questions.explanation: Cal first thinks of carnations (Nails of Love) but corrects himself toward marigolds (Nails of Death), then notes, "I'm beginning to think like Aron." The vacillation dramatizes his unresolved feelings—an impulse toward love checked by a starker reckoning with death—rather than full forgiveness, revenge, or rejection.

questions.question: The jade button Lee gives Abra is carved with "a human right hand, a lovely hand, the fingers curved and in repose," and was his mother's only ornament. In what way does this gift function as a symbol in the chapter, and how does the text show its emotional weight? Make a claim and support it with specific details.
questions.modelAnswer: The jade hand symbolizes Lee's act of choosing Abra as his true family and passing on something sacred and intimate—his dead mother's sole ornament. The text shows its weight through Lee's loss of composure: he chokes, grips his hands together, and admits "My Oriental calm seems to have deserted me," and through Abra's reverent handling—she moistens it, moves it over her lips, and presses the cool stone to her cheek. The hand "in repose" suggests peace and acceptance, mirroring the new familial bond sealed when Abra kisses Lee's cheek, the only time such a thing ever happened in his life. The gift converts their spoken wish ("I wish you were my daughter" / "I wish you were my father") into a concrete, inherited token of love.
questions.explanation: A strong answer must (1) identify the button as a symbol of chosen family and inherited love, (2) cite concrete textual evidence such as it being "my mother's only ornament," Lee's emotional breakdown and his use of the word "darling," or Abra's tender physical response, and (3) connect it to the father-daughter relationship the two have just declared. Vague answers that only say it is "a nice gift" without textual grounding fall short.

questions.question: Lee reflects that Cal "burning the money to punish himself" did not hurt as badly as "the crime," and that Cal is "crammed full to the top with every good thing and every bad thing," adding that "one single person could almost with the weight of a finger—." How does this chapter connect to the novel's good-versus-evil and timshel (the freedom to choose) throughline? Make a claim and support it with evidence from the chapter.
questions.modelAnswer: The chapter dramatizes timshel by showing characters actively choosing their moral direction rather than being fixed by inheritance. Cal carries "every good thing and every bad thing," and Lee's unfinished thought that "one single person could almost with the weight of a finger" tip the balance casts Abra as the loving influence that may help Cal choose good. Abra herself enacts a choice by burning Aron's letters and abandoning the demand to be perfect so she can be genuinely good. Even Lee's musing on moral balance—Adam on stolen money, Aron on whorehouse profits—frames goodness and evil as forces to be weighed and chosen, not inherited fates, which is the heart of the timshel idea.
questions.explanation: A strong answer should (1) make a claim that the chapter advances the timshel/choice theme, (2) cite specific evidence such as Cal being "crammed full" of good and bad, the "weight of a finger" line implying Abra can tip him toward good, or Abra choosing goodness over perfection by burning the letters, and (3) tie these to the idea that moral character is chosen rather than determined. Answers that merely summarize plot without engaging the choice/balance theme are insufficient.

chapters.summary: A late, cold spring delays the azalea blossoms Cal had chosen as the signal for his picnic with Abra, while Adam slowly recovers from his stroke under Lee's devoted 'conversational therapy.' When the azaleas finally bloom, Cal and Abra slip away to the Alisal; Abra takes Cal's hand and, to ease his belief that he attracts only bad things, confides that her own father is in disgrace for embezzling. Meanwhile a telegram arrives that Lee, dreading the news, finally opens, then prepares a glass of bromide for Adam as the chapter ends.

questions.question: What natural sign had Cal designated as the signal for when his picnic with Abra would take place?
options: The blooming of the wild azaleas in the Alisal
The first earth-warming sun of the year
The grain coming up in the Salinas Valley
The arrival of the spring wildflowers
questions.explanation: The text states 'Cal hadn't known it would be like this when he had made azaleas the signal for his picnic, but once the symbol was set it could not be violated.' When Lee urges him to go anyway, Cal insists, 'I can't. I said azaleas.' The other options are weather and season details mentioned in the chapter but are not the chosen signal.

questions.question: What does Lee call the method he uses to keep Adam calm and engaged while monitoring his recovery?
options: Conversational therapy
Absorption therapy
Blood-pressure betting
Cerebral pathology
questions.explanation: When Dr. Murphy asks how Lee keeps Adam from getting excited, Lee replies, 'It's my own invention... I call it conversational therapy.' The blood-pressure betting is one playful tactic within his care, 'absorption' is the medical process Lee describes to the doctor, and 'cerebral pathology' is the field Lee has studied, but only 'conversational therapy' is the name he gives his method.

questions.question: Why does Abra reveal that her own father is in trouble for taking money from his company?
options: To counter Cal's belief that he alone attracts bad things, by showing she carries trouble too
To explain why she has been distant and cannot stay for the picnic
To ask Cal for help in paying back the money her father stole
To warn Cal that her family may have to move away from Salinas
questions.explanation: Abra confronts Cal's self-pity ('You think you attract bad things—') and answers it with 'Well, I'm going to tell you something. My father's in trouble,' concluding 'You see, you're not the only one—.' Her purpose is solidarity, not an excuse to leave, a request for money, or a warning about relocation, none of which the text supports.

questions.question: Lee spends the afternoon listening anxiously for something before the doorbell rings. What does this growing dread foreshadow?
options: The bad news contained in the telegram he is afraid to open
A recurrence of Adam's cerebral hemorrhage
The discovery that he left the teakettle on the gas
An unwanted visit from Dr. Murphy
questions.explanation: Lee says of the doorbell, 'That's what I was listening for,' and his dread climaxes when he opens the telegram, turning it message-down and saying 'Nobody has the right to remove any single experience from another.' His fear is the telegram's news; the teakettle is a thought he dismisses ('No—I remember'), recurrence is a separate worry he voiced to the doctor earlier, and no visit from Murphy occurs.

questions.question: After opening the telegram, Lee measures bromide into a glass and prepares it before Adam comes home. What does this action most strongly imply?
options: The telegram brings grievous news, and Lee is steadying Adam against a shock
Lee intends to sedate himself to cope with his own grief
Adam's blood pressure has spiked and needs immediate medication
Lee is testing whether Adam will notice the change in his routine
questions.explanation: Lee, having read the telegram and pocketed it, prepares the pink bromide and waits with a now-steady hand as Adam fumbles at the door. The bromide is a calming agent readied for Adam, signaling Lee must deliver terrible news gently. Nothing indicates Lee drinks it himself, that Adam's pressure spiked, or that this is a test of routine.

questions.question: How does Steinbeck's choice to interrupt the picnic narrative with a factual account of the American assault on Cantigny shape the chapter's meaning?
options: It places the characters' private fears within the looming shadow of the war
It establishes Cal's eventual enlistment as a soldier in the First Division
It celebrates American heroism as a hopeful contrast to the family's troubles
It explains the cold weather as a direct result of the artillery in France
questions.explanation: The Cantigny section, dated May 28, 1918, drops a documentary war report into the intimate domestic story, framing the tender picnic and Lee's dread against the war that the chapter's dreaded telegram makes personal. It does not concern Cal enlisting, is too clinical to read as celebratory contrast, and the weather-from-guns idea is only a folk superstition the townspeople 'repeated,' not Steinbeck's claim.

questions.question: In what way does Abra's confession about her father complicate the novel's idea of inherited guilt, and how does her exchange with Cal at the stream show it? Make a claim and support it with specific details from the chapter.
questions.modelAnswer: Cal believes he is uniquely tainted, telling himself he 'attracts bad things,' a fear rooted in his sense of inherited evil. Abra punctures this by revealing her own father has 'taken some money from his company' and may go to jail, then saying 'you're not the only one—.' Her confession reframes guilt and shame as common human inheritances rather than Cal's special curse, offering connection instead of isolation. The detail that she takes his hand first and admits 'Now I'm afraid' shows she shares his burden willingly, making the moment about mutual humanity rather than damnation.
questions.explanation: Abra's disclosure directly answers Cal's self-pitying 'You think you attract bad things—' with evidence that trouble and disgrace are widespread, loosening the grip of the inherited-guilt theme. Acceptable answers should cite Cal's belief that he attracts bad things, Abra's revelation of her father's embezzlement, and her line 'you're not the only one' or 'Now I'm afraid' as evidence of shared rather than singular guilt.

questions.question: Evaluate Lee's decision to open the telegram rather than ignore it, and what his reasoning reveals about his moral character. Make a claim and support it with his words from the chapter.
questions.modelAnswer: Lee chooses to face the telegram despite naming himself 'a cowardly yellow belly,' showing courage precisely because he acts against his fear. His reasoning—'Nobody has the right to remove any single experience from another. Life and death are promised. We have a right to pain'—reveals a moral conviction that suffering is part of a full human life and must not be evaded or denied to others. By preparing the bromide for Adam afterward, he extends that ethic into compassionate action, accepting pain himself so he can buffer it for the man he loves. His choice embodies the novel's insistence that confronting hard truth, however terrible, is a moral act of love and responsibility.
questions.explanation: Lee battles his own dread, spitting at the envelope and calling himself a coward, yet opens it because he believes 'We have a right to pain.' Acceptable answers should reference his self-accusation of cowardice, his stated belief that no one may remove another's experience or right to pain, and his preparation of the bromide to soften the blow for Adam as evidence of his moral courage and care.

chapters.summary: A telegram brings news that Aron is dead and the shock has given Adam a paralyzing stroke; Cal, told he is now head of the family, confesses to his nearly helpless father that he caused both deaths by taking Aron to Kate's. Devastated and tempted toward self-destruction, Cal is sent by Lee to Abra, who refuses to flee with him and instead leads him back to Adam's house. There Lee pleads with the dying Adam to bless his guilt-ridden son rather than crush him, and Adam, with enormous effort, lifts his hand and whispers the single word 'Timshel.'

questions.question: What two pieces of news does the telegram and its aftermath deliver at the opening of the chapter?
options: That Aron is dead and that Adam has had a stroke
That Aron is wounded and that Cal has been drafted
That Kate is dead and that Adam is dying of grief
That Adam has died and that Aron is missing in action
questions.explanation: Lee tells Cal directly, 'Your brother is dead... Your father has had a stroke,' and explains that after he read Adam the telegram about Aron, the news 'seemed to get through into his brain and to explode there.' Adam is paralyzed but alive, and Kate's death is not announced in this chapter.

questions.question: According to Dr. Murphy, what is Adam's condition after the stroke?
options: He is nearly helpless, his left side paralyzed and right side partly affected, though he can talk a little with difficulty
He is fully conscious and expected to recover completely within a week
He is in a coma from which he will never wake again
He has lost his memory entirely but retains full movement
questions.explanation: Dr. Murphy explains the stroke is 'a leakage of blood in the brain,' that 'the left side is paralyzed. The right side partly,' that there is probably no sight in the left eye, and that 'your father is nearly helpless.' He adds Adam can talk 'A little—with difficulty,' and might live from a night to two years.

questions.question: When Cal insists that Adam's eyes have accused him of murder, how does Lee try to undercut that interpretation?
options: He argues that what Cal sees may be pressure on the brain area governing sight, not a deliberate accusation, since Adam earlier couldn't read for the same reason
He insists Adam is unconscious and his eyes are simply open by reflex
He claims Adam never loved Aron and so could not blame Cal for his death
He says Adam is only pretending to be ill in order to punish Cal
questions.explanation: Lee reasons, 'Adam's brain centers are affected. Anything you see in his eyes may be pressure on that part of his brain which governs his seeing... he couldn't read. That wasn't his eyes—that was pressure. You don't know he accused you.' Lee is trying to free Cal from reading guilt into Adam's involuntary gaze.

questions.question: When Cal tells Abra his mother was a whore and that he carries her tainted blood, how does Abra respond?
options: She answers that her own father is a thief and that she carries his blood, matching his shame with her own
She recoils and tells him she can no longer bear to be near him
She insists that blood does not exist and that he is only imagining his guilt
She agrees that his bad blood makes him dangerous and dooms him
questions.explanation: When Cal says 'my mother was a whore,' Abra replies 'I know. You told me. My father is a thief.' When he insists 'I've got her blood,' she answers 'I've got his.' She refuses to let inherited shame separate them, offering her own inheritance as a parallel rather than recoiling.

questions.question: Why does Abra refuse to go inside the willow tree with Cal and instead lead him back to Adam's house?
options: Because she will not join him while he is running away, and she insists he face his father rather than flee
Because she is afraid of being caught by her parents near the tree
Because she has decided she no longer loves him and wants to end things
Because she believes the willow tree belongs to Aron's memory and is sacred
questions.explanation: When Cal asks her into the willow tree, Abra says 'No... That's not right,' and 'Not if you're running away—no, I don't.' She then declares 'We're going back... To your father's house.' Her refusal is moral, aimed at making Cal confront rather than escape his situation.

questions.question: What does Lee mean by his extended image of the craftsman who keeps firing his clay to make a 'perfect cup'?
options: That every person in every generation is 'refired'—continually reshaped and given new chances—so the force that made humanity never stops trying to perfect it
That human beings are doomed slag, ruined by the fire and beyond saving
That only a few rare people, like fine porcelain, are worth saving while most are discarded
That suffering is meaningless and the kiln's fire destroys without any purpose
questions.explanation: Lee renounces his old belief that the good are destroyed and that he merely 'inherited' scars and impurities, then offers a new view: 'every man in every generation is refired,' all impurities burned out 'ready for a glorious flux, and for that—more fire,' aiming at 'perfection.' He asks, 'Can you think that whatever made us—would stop trying?'—affirming renewal, not doom.

questions.question: Evaluate Lee's decision to tell the dying, nearly helpless Adam exactly what Cal did, despite Cal's protest that he 'can't' and the risk that the truth might kill him. What claim does the chapter make through this choice, and how does the text support it?
questions.modelAnswer: Lee deliberately forces the full truth on Adam—'He did a thing in anger... The result of his anger is that his brother and your son is dead'—because he believes Cal must not be 'left alone with his guilt' or crushed by rejection. The chapter frames Lee's gamble as an act of love and moral courage: he says 'If it kills him I have to. I have the choice,' echoing the timshel idea that a person can choose. A strong answer takes a defensible position and cites Lee's insistence on choice and his plea 'Don't crush him with rejection,' noting that the confrontation produces Adam's blessing rather than his destruction.

questions.question: The chapter ends with Adam whispering 'Timshel!' How does this single word resolve the novel's good-versus-evil and inheritance throughline for Cal? Make a claim and support it with specific details from the chapter.
questions.modelAnswer: Adam's 'Timshel'—meaning 'thou mayest'—blesses Cal by insisting he is not doomed by his inherited 'bad blood' or his guilt but is free to choose his own goodness. The word answers Cal's terror that his mother's blood and his father's accusing eyes condemn him ('I've got her blood... There's nowhere I can go to get away'). It also fulfills Lee's plea, 'Let him be free. That's all a man has over the beasts,' and pairs with Lee's vision that every generation is 'refired.' A strong answer claims the word grants Cal moral freedom against determinism and cites Adam's struggle to lift his hand and form the word as the deliberate aim of Lee's pleading.
